danger_signals.visual_form: water bodies surrounding fairways and greens
danger_signals.meaning: penalty zone; ball loss or stroke penalty if ball enters water

danger_signals.visual_form: sand traps visually distinct from fairway
danger_signals.meaning: difficulty zone; requires recovery shot with sand wedge
physical obstruction; ball may be blocked from reaching target
reduced ball control; longer grass impedes distance

safety_signals.visual_form: open mowed grass area between tee and green
safety_signals.meaning: optimal play zone; full distance control available
final scoring zone; precision putting mechanics activate

safety_signals.visual_form: elevated starting platform at hole beginning
safety_signals.meaning: controlled launch point; maximum distance potential available

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: course overview (overhead view) available in World Class edition; allows player assessment of hazard placement and layout before play
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player can plan strategy based on visual course topology

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: visual complexity increases across courses; Cypress Creek, Doral Country Club, St Andrews, and Gauntlet Country Club present escalating architectural challenges
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player adapts club selection and shot strategy based on visual hazard density

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: printable scorecard displays cumulative strokes; visual feedback on ball position relative to hole
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player adjusts risk tolerance based on score visualization

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: punch shot mechanic available; ball trajectory shown from overhead perspective
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player visualizes and executes alternative shot angles to navigate obstacles

environmental_storytelling.example: inclusion of famous courses (Cypress Creek, Doral Country Club, St Andrews) communicates authenticity and prestige; player understands they are competing on recognized championship venues
Gauntlet Country Club as fictional venue signals an artificial test of extreme difficulty; visual design likely emphasizes hazard concentration to communicate challenge tier
Executive Edition added trees and bunkers to original water-only hazard model; landscape evolution communicates increasing simulation realism and course complexity
course editor allowing modification of existing layouts communicates player agency in world shaping; visual changes directly reflect strategic decisions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: trajectory visualization from overhead and side perspectives; ball physics respond to club selection, power meter reading, and environmental factors (wind, terrain slope)
putting mechanics activate; ball movement becomes precision-based; visual green slope indicators communicate break direction
positioned at tee box or ball location; awaits club selection and shot input
ball collision with water results in visual disappearance and stroke penalty; RealSound audio feedback reinforces penalty state

notes: World Class Leader Board Golf (1992, Genesis) is a golf simulation with limited faction/political/danger communication systems typical of sports titles. Visual world-state communication centers on course topology, hazard placement, and ball physics feedback. The game lacks narrative danger signals or faction identity systems. Manifestation occurs primarily through environmental design (fairway vs. rough vs. hazard), scoring visualization, and course-specific architectural features. The shift from water-only hazards (1986) to inclusion of bunkers and trees (Executive/World Class editions) represents progression in visual complexity as world-state communicator. No color-coded faction systems or political indicators present.
